figures.gloss: In Mesoamerican thought, the self is doubled: the tonal — the day-sign soul, one's calendrical destiny and waking person — and the nagual, the hidden animal co-essence, or the specialist able to cross into it and travel apart from the body.
figures.facet: The best-documented Mesoamerican answer to what else a human is: a public calendrical person set beside a hidden animal counterpart — the schema the famous forgery imitated.
figures.provenance: Nahua tonalli in Alfredo López Austin, 'The Human Body and Ideology' (1980); nagualism surveyed in George Foster, 'Nagualism in Mexico and Guatemala' (1944); colonial attestation in Ruiz de Alarcón's 'Treatise' (1629). Distinct from Castaneda's fabricated version (the forgery node); Horden's modern Toltec I Ching synthesis rides the yin-yang bridge edge, not this entry's pedigree.

figures.gloss: The rubber-ball game played on an I-shaped court: a cosmic contest of life against death, sun against underworld, enacted with human bodies — and sometimes settled with human blood.
figures.facet: Not a metaphor performed but a rite enacted: at this game the Hero Twins out-played death itself and rose as the sun and the moon.
figures.provenance: The cosmic ballgame and the Hero-Twins myth are firmly attested (Popol Vuh). A distinct game from patolli — the athletic court, not the four-directions board.

figures.gloss: The Aztec cosmological history of successive world-ages, each ruled by a sun and destroyed by a matching cataclysm — jaguars devouring a race of giants, a hurricane, a rain of fire, a flood — before the present Fifth Sun (4-Movement) was kindled at Teotihuacan, fated to end by earthquake.
figures.facet: It turns the roster's Tezcatlipoca–Quetzalcoatl rivalry and Huitzilopochtli's demand for blood into chapters of one recurring pattern: creation only ever holds provisionally, against a name and a date already set for its ending.
figures.provenance: Leyenda de los Soles (Codex Chimalpopoca, 1558); Historia de los Mexicanos por sus Pinturas; Sahagún's Florentine Codex, Book 7; synthesized in H.B. Nicholson, 'Religion in Pre-Hispanic Central Mexico' (1971).

figures.gloss: The disfigured, pustuled god who, when the haughty Tecciztecatl hesitates, leaps first into the divine hearth at Teotihuacan and rises transfigured as the sun of the Fifth Age.
figures.facet: Humility completes the cosmos where pride fails it — and the sun that results is not free but a debtor, immediately demanding the blood that keeps it moving across the sky.
figures.provenance: Leyenda de los Soles (1558); Sahagún's Florentine Codex, Book 7, ch. 2.

figures.gloss: The great Classic-era metropolis — never Aztec-built, already a millennium-old ruin by the Mexica period — that the Aztecs revered as 'the place where the gods were made,' the site of the Fifth Sun's creation and the source of the earliest Feathered Serpent temple.
figures.facet: It is the composite's actual connective tissue: a real city the Aztecs themselves quoted as sacred history, giving 'Olmec → Maya → Aztec' a physical anchor rather than an asserted arc.
figures.provenance: Sahagún's Florentine Codex, Book 10 (Aztec veneration of the ruins); archaeology of the Feathered Serpent Pyramid (Sugiyama excavations); León-Portilla on Teotihuacan's afterlife in Mexica cosmology.

figures.gloss: 'Our Lord the Flayed One,' god of spring renewal, seed-corn and goldsmiths, whose priests wore the flayed skins of sacrificed captives at the Tlacaxipehualiztli festival as the earth wears new vegetation over the old.
figures.facet: He is the Red Tezcatlipoca of the four-brothers quartet — the sky-quartering cosmology's east-facing member, missing until now from the roster that already names him.
figures.provenance: Sahagún's Florentine Codex, Book 2 (Tlacaxipehualiztli); H.B. Nicholson on the four Tezcatlipocas.

figures.gloss: The moon-associated warrior daughter of Coatlicue who led her four hundred brothers against their pregnant mother at Coatepec, only to be decapitated and dismembered by the newborn Huitzilopochtli — her body's fall re-enacted afterward on captives rolled down the Templo Mayor's steps.
figures.facet: Her carved, dismembered stone lay at Huitzilopochtli's temple foot as a permanent diagram of what happens to any challenge to Mexica solar-military order.
figures.provenance: Sahagún's Florentine Codex, Book 3; the Coyolxauhqui monolith (Templo Mayor, excavated 1978); Elizabeth Boone on the Coatepec myth cycle.

figures.gloss: The ravenous earth-monster, torn in two by Quetzalcoatl and Tezcatlipoca to make sky and earth; her body sprouts the world's trees and grasses, and she must go on being fed blood in recompense for the tearing.
figures.facet: She completes the sky-quartering the four Tezcatlipocas perform with an earth-creation of equal violence — the ground itself is a slain and still-hungry body, not neutral matter.
figures.provenance: Historia de los Mexicanos por sus Pinturas; Codex Borgia and Codex Vaticanus B iconography (Tlaltecuhtli carved face-down on monument undersides); Cecelia Klein on Tlaltecuhtli imagery.

figures.gloss: The 260-day divinatory count of 13 numbers by 20 signs, kept without interruption from Olmec-era origins through the Maya tzolk'in to the Aztec tonalpohualli, fixing names, fates, and the calendar of feast-days across two millennia of Mesoamerican cultures.
figures.facet: It is the one demonstrably pan-Mesoamerican inheritance in the whole composite — exactly the kind of continuity a composite tradition exists to hold, and the frame the roster's own patolli board is a physical circuit of.
figures.provenance: Munro Edmonson, 'The Book of the Year' (1988); Prudence Rice on Maya-Aztec calendar continuity; earliest Long Count/260-day inscriptions from Olmec-sphere sites (e.g. Chiapa de Corzo Stela 2).

edges.note: The Twins win their lives by out-playing death at the court — the founding match of the Popol Vuh.
William Douglas Horden's The Toltec I Ching joins the Mesoamerican tonal/nagual to the Chinese yin/yang — a real synthesis from a real lineage, the join this whole project is named for.
The famous version is fabricated. Its seam is aimed deliberately back at the grounded Toltec line it imitated — the fake set beside the real so the difference can be seen.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The generation of life and cosmic continuation through highly orchestrated, high-stakes ritual collision that demands a blood price.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both systems partition the human entity into distinct, operating spiritual and animal components that interact across different planes of reality.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both systems feature an intimate, inextricably linked spiritual double or divine counterpart that the practitioner must integrate with to achieve ultimate realization.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both systems mathematically divide time to map birth horoscopes, determining an individual's fate and psychological composition based on calendrical geometry.
A recurring ritual that re-enacts the founding passage through death to renew the covenant of the world; a distant structural parallel, flagged.
GOLD SEAM: A vital spirit double or co-essence that is intimately tied to the practitioner's fate, power, and spiritual maturation.

Ritual re-enactment as cosmic maintenance: the purulli recitation of the serpent's defeat and the Mesoamerican ballgame both stage a mythic combat to keep the world's order turning — independent inventions of the same logic.

GOLD SEAM: the chi and the Mesoamerican tonal are both a destiny-companion-spirit bound to a person from birth — a portion of self carried in the unseen. Independent invention across families; both seam to the proposed personal-daimon joint.
The shaman's detachable soul that journeys and can be lost rhymes with the Mesoamerican nagual — the self that travels apart from the body.

Both involve the idea of personal destiny and choice — but reshut is the free will to choose good or evil, while tonal is the day-soul that shapes one's fate. A loose parallel of human agency, flagged.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A loose parallel where one's own life force is inextricably bound and conditionally linked to the fate of a superior or external entity.
GOLD SEAM: Lethal, highly choreographed athletic rituals performed in central courtyards that act as cosmic sacraments rather than mere public sports.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both traditions posit an inextricable spiritual and energetic link between a human being and a specific animal counterpart that provides vitality and protection.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Olmec 'were-animal' motif is the foundational root of the pan-Mesoamerican belief in animal spirit companions and shamanic transformation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The rulers depicted in the colossal heads wear the thick protective helmets utilized in the sacred Mesoamerican ballgame.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Extreme, potentially lethal physical athletic competition employed to resolve intense political and cosmic tensions among rival factions.

Both name a second, hidden register of the self that the practitioner crosses into — the San healer in trance and the Mesoamerican nagual-state — the everyday self set against its potent other. (Joe's truth-bar: the San dance is grounded ethnography; the kinship is structural, and the Mesoamerican side carries its own forgery caveat.)
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A binary reality model where the visible, material world is viewed as a dependent shadow of underlying, invisible spiritual forces.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A structural parallel where the practitioner maps and manipulates the fluid subjective realm (Nagual) to force necessary changes in the fixed objective world (Tonal).

The animal-double joint reached independently in two American regions: the Tukano shaman's jaguar-self against the Mesoamerican nagual, the belief that a specialist's power is inseparable from a paired animal form.
Nanahuatzin's self-immolation at Teotihuacan is the act that kindles the Fifth Sun within this world-age sequence.
Teotihuacan is the named site of the Fifth Sun's creation — the ages have a geography, not just a sequence.
Tonatiuh is the reigning, Fifth chapter of this composite's world-age sequence — the specific deity of the present age the frame describes.
independently invented cosmologies of successive worlds destroyed and remade, each culminating in the present, precarious age.
vast cyclical ages of arising and destruction, structurally convergent across unrelated cosmological traditions.
Teotihuacan's Feathered Serpent Pyramid predates and iconographically anchors the later Aztec cult of Quetzalcoatl, whose ruins the Mexica revered as the birthplace of the gods.
Xipe Totec is the Red Tezcatlipoca of the East, one of the four sons who quarter the cosmos in the rostered entry that already names him.
The rebel sister dismembered at birth by the god she and her brothers marched against at Coatepec — the founding defeat behind his solar-warrior cult.
Daughter and mother at the center of the Coatepec myth: Coyolxauhqui leads the attack on pregnant Coatlicue that Huitzilopochtli is born armed to repel.
One of the pair of gods who tear her body in two to make sky and earth.
The other of the pair who tears her body in two — the earth-creation companion myth to the four-Tezcatlipocas' sky-quartering.
a primordial being whose dismemberment yields the literal fabric of the cosmos — earth and sky here, the ordered universe and society there.
The rostered patolli board's 52-space circuit is a game-form of the Calendar Round that the 260-day tonalpohualli anchors.
an independently invented mathematical division of time into a fixed cycle of named units, used to read birth-destiny and psychological fate.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: these sculptures are the concrete artistic evidence underlying the same Olmec-rooted, pan-Mesoamerican belief in animal spirit-companions and shamanic transformation already linked from the were-jaguar entry.

Ceremonial ball games played on purpose-built stone courts, carrying ritual and inter-polity weight — the Taíno batey and the Mesoamerican ōllamaliztli, a widespread ball-game complex across the Caribbean and Mesoamerica.

dossier.essence: The shared religious civilization of Mesoamerica — the cultural sphere running from the Olmec (from c. 1200 BCE) through the Classic and Postclassic civilizations to the Spanish Conquest of 1521 — treated here as the common substrate beneath the region's many local cults, principally Aztec and Maya religion, whose own gods, world-ages, and afterlife geography are catalogued in their own dossiers (aztec, maya). Across two and a half millennia and unrelated language families, Mesoamerican peoples shared an interlocking sacred calendar, a doubled conception of the self, and a ritual ballgame that staged cosmic combat with the human body.
dossier.cosmos: The clearest evidence of a genuinely pan-Mesoamerican common stock is the calendar: the interlocking 260-day and 365-day counts (forming the 52-year Calendar Round) were kept, largely intact, across Olmec-descended societies from the Maya to the Aztec, and with them a doubled conception of the person — the tonal, the day-sign soul that is one's calendrical destiny and waking self, set beside the nagual, a hidden animal co-essence, or the specialist able to cross into it and travel apart from the body. The ballgame (Ollamaliztli), played across the region on its characteristic I-shaped court, staged this same civilization's central cosmic drama — life contending with death, the sun with the underworld — sometimes settled with human blood.
dossier.practice: A trained priesthood, attested across the region's societies, kept the calendars and divined by them, regulating naming, agriculture, and the festival cycle around the day-count; specialists in the doubled self read fate and diagnosed misfortune through it. The ballgame was played from monumental Classic-period courts down to household-scale versions, framed everywhere as ritual rather than mere sport. Public cult ended with the Conquest, but calendrical divination and nagualism persist in folk and highland tradition into the present.
dossier.canon: No pan-regional scripture: the shared calendar and ballgame are attested archaeologically (ballcourts and Olmec were-jaguar iconography from the Preclassic onward) and, for the doubled self, ethnographically in colonial and later sources (Ruiz de Alarcon's Treatise, 1629; George Foster's 20th-century survey of nagualism). The fullest narrative sources for the region's specific gods and myths — the Popol Vuh, Sahagun's Florentine Codex — belong to the Maya and Aztec traditions and are cited there.
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds the region's shared operating system beneath its many local pantheons: sacred time as the machinery of destiny, and a self doubled between a public calendrical face and a hidden animal other.
sources: Alfredo Lopez Austin, 'The Human Body and Ideology: Concepts of the Ancient Nahuas' (1988)
George Foster, 'Nagualism in Mexico and Guatemala' (1944)
